annotations.label: Law of large numbers (informal)

anchor.snippet: the law of large numbers is a mathematical law which states that the average of the results obtained from a large number of independent random samples converges to the true value

annotations.note: The informal LLN is captured by `strong_law_ae` (Banach-valued) and `strong_law_ae_real` (real-valued), which show `n⁻¹·∑ Xᵢ` converges to `𝔼[X₀]`.

annotations.label: Dice roll example

anchor.snippet: A single roll of a six-sided dice produces one of the numbers 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, or 6, each with equal probability

annotations.note: No dedicated dice-roll instance in Mathlib; while a uniform PMF on `Fin 6` could be built from existing infrastructure, no named example formalizes this scenario.

annotations.label: Bernoulli trials empirical probability

anchor.snippet: It follows from the law of large numbers that the empirical probability of success in a series of Bernoulli trials will converge to the theoretical probability

annotations.note: Mathlib has Bernoulli distributions (e.g. `Mathlib.Probability.Distributions.SetBernoulli`) and the strong law subsumes this corollary, but the Bernoulli-specific empirical-probability statement is not stated.

annotations.label: Fair coin toss

anchor.snippet: a fair coin toss is a Bernoulli trial

annotations.note: No specific fair-coin example is formalized; generic Bernoulli distribution infrastructure exists but a named coin-toss instance does not.

annotations.label: Absolute difference grows

anchor.snippet: Although the proportion of heads (and tails) approaches 1 ⁄ 2 , almost surely the absolute difference in the number of heads and tails will become large as the number of flips becomes large

annotations.note: This consequence of CLT/random-walk behavior for fair coin flips is not stated in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Monte Carlo method

anchor.snippet: Another good example of the law of large numbers is the Monte Carlo method

annotations.note: The only Mathlib mention of Monte Carlo is in a bibliography comment in `Mathlib.Probability.Kernel.Irreducible`; no formal algorithm/theorem is provided.

annotations.label: Cauchy/Pareto non-convergence

anchor.snippet: The average of the results obtained from a large number of trials may fail to converge in some cases

annotations.note: Mathlib defines the Cauchy distribution in `Mathlib.Probability.Distributions.Cauchy` but does not formalize failure of LLN for heavy-tailed distributions.

annotations.label: Sample average converges to expected value

anchor.snippet: both versions of the law state that the sample average

annotations.note: `strong_law_ae` states that the sample average `n⁻¹ • ∑ i ∈ range n, X i` converges to `μ[X 0]`.

annotations.label: Weak law of large numbers (Khinchin)

anchor.snippet: The weak law of large numbers (also called Khinchin 's law) states that given a collection of independent and identically distributed (iid) samples from a random variable with finite mean, the sample mean converges in probability to the expected value

annotations.note: No standalone `weak_law` exists in Mathlib; the weak law follows from `strong_law_ae` (via `tendstoInMeasure_of_tendsto_ae`, as used inside `strong_law_Lp`) but is not stated as a named theorem.

annotations.label: Chebyshev's bounded variance variant

anchor.snippet: If the variances are bounded, then the law applies, as shown by Chebyshev as early as 1867

annotations.note: Mathlib has Chebyshev's inequality (`meas_ge_le_variance_div_sq`) which is the standard tool for this version of the weak law, but the bounded-variance weak law itself is not packaged as a named theorem.

annotations.label: Strong law of large numbers (Kolmogorov)

anchor.snippet: The strong law of large numbers (also called Kolmogorov 's law) states that the sample average converges almost surely to the expected value

annotations.note: `strong_law_ae` (and its real specialization `strong_law_ae_real`) formalizes a.s. convergence of the sample mean to `𝔼[X 0]`, in fact Etemadi's version requiring only pairwise independence.

annotations.label: Kolmogorov's strong law (non-identical)

anchor.snippet: If the summands are independent but not identically distributed

annotations.note: Mathlib's `strong_law_ae` requires `IdentDistrib (X i) (X 0)`; the non-iid Kolmogorov criterion `∑ Var(Xₖ)/bₖ² < ∞` is not formalized.

annotations.label: Exponential variable with no Lebesgue expectation

anchor.section: Differences between the weak law and the strong law
anchor.snippet: Let X be an exponentially distributed random variable with parameter 1

annotations.note: Although `Mathlib.Probability.Distributions.Exponential` defines the distribution, this counterexample illustrating absence of Lebesgue expectation is not formalized.

annotations.label: Geometric variable without conventional expectation

anchor.section: Differences between the weak law and the strong law
anchor.snippet: Let X be a geometrically distributed random variable with probability 0.5

annotations.note: `Mathlib.Probability.Distributions.Geometric` defines the distribution, but this specific counterexample about non-existent expectation is not formalized.

annotations.label: Plus/minus sequence where strong law fails

anchor.section: Differences between the weak law and the strong law
anchor.snippet: Kolmogorov's strong law does not apply because the partial sum in his criterion

annotations.note: This counterexample to Kolmogorov's non-iid criterion is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Uniform law of large numbers

anchor.snippet: A particular example of a uniform law of large numbers states the conditions under which the convergence happens uniformly in

annotations.note: No uniform LLN (convergence uniform in a parameter, used for empirical-process/statistical-learning theory) is present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Borel's law of large numbers

anchor.snippet: Borel's law of large numbers , named after Émile Borel , states that if an experiment is repeated a large number of times, independently under identical conditions, then the proportion of times that any specified event is expected to occur approximately equals the probability of the event's occurrence on any particular trial

annotations.note: Borel's LLN (indicator-variable special case) is not stated as such but is a direct consequence of `strong_law_ae_real` applied to indicators; no named Borel-LLN theorem exists.

annotations.label: Histogram from law of large numbers

anchor.snippet: With this method, one can cover the whole x-axis with a grid (with grid size 2h) and obtain a bar graph which is called a histogram

annotations.note: No histogram/density-estimation construction tied to LLN is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Monte Carlo integration algorithm

anchor.snippet: One application of the law of large numbers is an important method of approximation known as the Monte Carlo method

annotations.note: Mathlib has no formalization of Monte Carlo integration; only a bibliographic reference appears in `Mathlib.Probability.Kernel.Irreducible`.
